story:
The first time they bump shoulders, she’s gripping a bouquet and he’s escorting a bride down the aisle. She can feel his gaze; she blushes the shade of her peonies and keeps that Mona Lisa half-grin she’s worn since morning, the smile that’s made her face ache. She’s already dreaming of tiny hotel bottles and a hot shower and frowning at herself in the mirror just to stretch the cramped muscles the other way.

You don’t flirt with the father of the bride. Everyone knows that. Or the uncle. Or any relation. But this man isn’t family. He’s a stand‑in—someone said coworker, boss, maybe a mechanic—because the bride’s estranged from her people.

Why is Wilma here, anyway? She barely knows the bride. She knows the groom. Barney. Not in a wink-wink way, but also not not. Once upon a costume party they almost hooked up—she went as Wilma Flintstone (obvious), he as Barney Rubble (also obvious), and the taboo—her cartoon husband’s best friend—had a spark. But they were too drunk, and the window slid shut.

They became friends instead. The kind of friendship everyone warns you can’t really exist, because someone will always feel a pulse. Maybe so. Still, they were study-close, picnic-close, almost‑but‑not‑quite-spark-close. In the Friend Zone, thank you very much, naysayers.

Before he got engaged, Barney showed up for her. One A.M., cardboard boxes, a borrowed pickup, a break‑up move. Later, he found her sobbing in the office bathroom after a two-night stand went sideways, dried her off, drove her to the pier, fed seagulls, reframed the world. They were like couples in those ads with a scroll of side effects: easy together.

“Why aren’t you dating?” her best friend asked.

“Because he’s Barney,” she said, as if the name explained everything. Maybe it did.

He insisted she be a bridesmaid. If not, he joked, he’d put her with the groomsmen. So here she stands, flanked by pretty maids who seem determined to dissuade and shade her. Her dress is wet paper bag brown—hers alone—while the women beside her wear chocolate and taupe. If the bride doesn’t hate her, why this color?

The faux father keeps smiling. He’s good-looking. Dark hair. Blue eyes. A bit hot, honestly. Does she look beautiful or ridiculous? Wilma is lightheaded, from too little sleep and too many airplane vodkas. Her gift to them: a gravy boat, the most 1950s thing she could find. Fitting, because Betty—Barney’s one and only—feels 1950s to her. Doilies incoming.

The church is heavy with gardenia and peony. She wants soap, water, freedom. She’s dehydrated by design, terrified of needing the bathroom mid-ceremony. A wasp taps a stained-glass corner like it wants out as badly as she does. Maybe it loves Barney too.

Then the priest says those movie words—speak now—and the room tilts. Maybe she does love him. Maybe she’s been playing Wilma when she wanted to be Betty, with doilies and a gravy boat.

Her knees go; the rest follows. The stand‑in lunges, catches her, carries her outside like a groom over a threshold to a meaning nobody remembers. Shade. Breeze. Water. Her dress darkens at the bodice and she doesn’t care. He waits, worried, then leans against a tree and finally offers his hand.

“I don’t think we’ve been properly introduced. My name is Fred.”

“Wilma,” she says, and smiles.
